title: Hot and Sour Soup

seo.title: Authentic Hot and Sour Soup Recipe - Easy & Delicious
seo.metaDescription: Master the art of authentic Hot and Sour Soup with our easy-to-follow recipe. Get that perfect balance of spicy, tangy, and savory flavors at home. A Chinese classic!

seo.imageAlt: A steaming bowl of authentic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup garnished with green onions and chili oil, ready to be enjoyed.
seo.imageCaption: Warm up with a bowl of this classic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup, bursting with spicy, tangy, and umami flavors.

description: Hot and Sour Soup is a beloved Chinese classic, known for its invigorating blend of spicy heat and tangy vinegar. This comforting soup features a rich broth filled with tender pork, delicate tofu, and earthy mushrooms, making it a perfect appetizer or light meal. Each spoonful offers a harmonious balance of flavors that truly awakens the palate.

ingredients.notes: mixed with 1/4 cup cold water for slurry

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: Rehydrate dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in warm water for 20 minutes; drain and slice. Slice pork thinly, then julienne tofu and bamboo shoots. Prepare your cornstarch slurry.

instructions.title: Sauté Aromatics and Meat
instructions.text: In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat a tablespoon of cooking oil over medium-high heat. Add the sliced pork and stir-fry until it is lightly browned, about 3-4 minutes. Add the sliced mushrooms and julienned bamboo shoots, stir-fry for another 2-3 minutes.

instructions.title: Simmer the Broth
instructions.text: Pour in the chicken broth, soy sauce, rice vinegar, and white pepper. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil, then reduce the heat to a simmer. Let it cook gently for 10 minutes to allow all the flavors to fully meld together.

instructions.title: Add Tofu and Thicken
instructions.text: Gently add the tofu matchsticks to the simmering soup. Stir the cornstarch slurry one last time to ensure it is well mixed, then slowly pour it into the soup while continuously stirring. Continue stirring until the soup thickens to your desired consistency.

instructions.title: Create Egg Ribbons
instructions.text: Once the soup has thickened, remove it from direct heat or lower the heat to very low. While stirring the soup in a slow, circular motion, slowly drizzle in the beaten egg. This technique creates delicate, wispy egg ribbons throughout the soup.

instructions.title: Season and Serve
instructions.text: Stir in the toasted sesame oil and chili oil (if using, for extra heat and flavor). Taste the soup and adjust the seasoning as needed, adding more rice vinegar or white pepper for extra tang or spice. Ladle the hot soup into individual bowls and garnish generously with fresh chopped green onions before serving immediately.

tips: For authentic flavor, do not skimp on the white pepper and rice vinegar; these are key to the hot and sour balance. Adjust to your personal preference.
If you prefer a vegetarian version, substitute pork with more mushrooms or rehydrated dried gluten, and use vegetable broth instead of chicken broth.
This soup is best enjoyed fresh. If reheating, add a splash of broth or water to thin if it has thickened too much, and adjust seasoning.
For extra depth of flavor, use the soaking liquid from the dried shiitake mushrooms (strained) as part of your broth.
Ensure your cornstarch slurry is well-mixed and added slowly to avoid lumps and achieve a smooth consistency.
article:
Hot and Sour Soup is more than just a dish; it’s an experience, a vibrant symphony of flavors that has captivated palates worldwide. This iconic Chinese soup, celebrated for its unique blend of savory, spicy, and tangy notes, is a cornerstone of Chinese cuisine. Whether you’re seeking warmth on a chilly evening, a flavorful appetizer to kick off a grand meal, or simply a bowl of comforting deliciousness, Hot and Sour Soup delivers every time. Its complex profile, featuring earthy mushrooms, tender tofu, crisp bamboo shoots, and a punchy broth, makes it an unforgettable culinary journey.

## What is Hot and Sour Soup?

At its heart, Hot and Sour Soup is a traditional Chinese soup characterized by its distinctive "hot" (spicy) and "sour" (tangy) flavor profile. The "hot" typically comes from white pepper, which offers a unique, pungent heat distinct from chili, often complemented by a touch of chili oil for extra warmth. The "sour" element is derived from vinegar, most commonly rice vinegar, which lends a bright, acidic counterpoint. Beyond these defining tastes, the soup is rich in umami, thanks to ingredients like soy sauce and dried mushrooms, creating a deeply satisfying base. Originating from Northern China, particularly Sichuan and Henan provinces, it’s a versatile dish that has evolved over centuries, with countless regional variations, each boasting its own subtle nuances. Despite the variations, the core essence – a harmonious balance of heat, tang, and savory depth – remains universally beloved.

## Ingredients for Authentic Hot and Sour Soup

The magic of Hot and Sour Soup lies in its carefully selected components, each playing a vital role in creating its signature taste and texture. To craft an authentic bowl, you'll need a combination of pantry staples and special Asian ingredients:

*   **Broth:** A good quality chicken or pork broth forms the foundation. For a vegetarian version, a rich vegetable broth works beautifully.
*   **Proteins:** Shredded lean pork or chicken are common, adding substance and savory depth. Firm or extra-firm tofu, cut into matchsticks, is essential for its silky texture and ability to absorb flavors.
*   **Mushrooms:** Dried wood ear mushrooms and dried shiitake mushrooms are crucial. Rehydrated and thinly sliced, they provide an earthy aroma and a delightful chewy texture.
*   **Bamboo Shoots:** Canned bamboo shoots, sliced into thin strips, offer a subtle crunch and a slightly sweet, earthy flavor.
*   **Eggs:** Whisked eggs, slowly drizzled into the simmering soup, create beautiful "egg flowers" that add richness and a delicate texture.
*   **Thickener:** A cornstarch slurry is used to give the soup its characteristic slightly viscous consistency, allowing the flavors to cling to each spoonful.
*   **Flavorings:**
    *   **Rice Vinegar:** The primary source of tang.
    *   **White Pepper:** Non-negotiable for the "hot" element; its sharp, distinct heat is key.
    *   **Soy Sauce:** Adds umami and savory depth.
    *   **Sesame Oil:** Drizzled in at the end for its nutty aroma.
    *   **Chili Oil (optional):** For an extra kick of spice.
*   **Garnish:** Freshly chopped green onions and cilantro brighten the soup with their fresh aroma and vibrant color.

## Step-by-Step: How to Make Hot and Sour Soup

Creating your own delicious Hot and Sour Soup at home is surprisingly straightforward. Follow these steps for a perfect bowl:

1.  **Preparation:** Rehydrate dried wood ear and shiitake mushrooms in warm water for at least 30 minutes, then drain, stem (for shiitake), and thinly slice. Slice tofu, bamboo shoots, and any chosen meat into thin matchsticks. Whisk eggs in a small bowl. In another small bowl, mix cornstarch with a few tablespoons of cold water to form a slurry.
2.  **Sauté Aromatics (Optional but Recommended):** In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat a little oil. Sauté minced garlic and ginger for about 30 seconds until fragrant.
3.  **Build the Broth:** Pour in the chicken or vegetable broth and bring it to a gentle simmer. Add the sliced meat (if using), mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and tofu. Cook for 5-7 minutes, or until the meat is cooked through.
4.  **Seasoning:** Stir in the soy sauce, rice vinegar, and a generous amount of white pepper. Taste and adjust the "hot" and "sour" balance to your preference. This is the stage to really make it your own.
5.  **Thicken the Soup:** Give the cornstarch slurry a quick re-stir, then slowly pour it into the simmering soup in a thin stream, stirring continuously. Continue to stir gently until the soup thickens to your desired consistency.
6.  **Add Egg Flowers:** Once the soup is thickened, reduce the heat to a very low simmer. Slowly drizzle the whisked eggs into the soup in a thin stream, stirring gently with chopsticks or a fork in a circular motion. This creates delicate strands of cooked egg.
7.  **Finishing Touches:** Remove the soup from the heat. Stir in a dash of sesame oil and, if desired, a swirl of chili oil. Taste one last time and adjust seasonings if needed.
8.  **Serve:** Ladle the Hot and Sour Soup into bowls and garnish generously with fresh green onions and cilantro.

## Pro Tips for the Best Hot and Sour Soup

Elevating your homemade Hot and Sour Soup from good to absolutely fantastic involves a few key techniques and considerations:

*   **Don't Skimp on White Pepper:** This is arguably the most crucial ingredient for the "hot" component. Black pepper is not a substitute. Be bold with white pepper; it provides that distinctive, pungent heat.
*   **Balance is Key:** Taste as you go, especially when adding vinegar and white pepper. These are the elements that define the soup, and personal preference plays a huge role. You can always add more, but you can't take it away.
*   **Perfect Egg Flowers:** For delicate, wispy egg strands, ensure your soup is at a gentle simmer (not a rolling boil) when you drizzle in the eggs. Pour slowly from a slight height and stir gently in one direction to create beautiful ribbons.
*   **Freshness at the Finish:** Add the rice vinegar and sesame oil towards the very end of cooking, or even directly into individual bowls. This preserves their volatile aromas and bright flavors, preventing them from cooking off.
*   **Quality Broth:** A rich, flavorful broth will significantly enhance the overall taste. If using store-bought, opt for a low-sodium version so you can control the saltiness.
*   **Garnish Generously:** Fresh green onions and cilantro aren't just for looks; they add a burst of freshness that brightens every spoonful.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Hot and Sour Soup is incredibly versatile, making it suitable for various dining occasions. It makes an excellent appetizer, perfectly whetting the appetite for a larger Chinese feast. Imagine starting your meal with a steaming bowl before diving into dishes like General Tso's chicken, Kung Pao shrimp, vegetable lo mein, or a classic fried rice.

Beyond being an appetizer, it can easily stand alone as a light, comforting meal. Pair it with some crispy spring rolls, savory potstickers, or even a simple side of steamed white rice for a satisfying lunch or dinner. On a cold day, a bowl of this soup is pure comfort food, warming you from the inside out. For beverages, a light Chinese tea like jasmine tea or a crisp lager beer complements its rich flavors beautifully.

## Hot and Sour Soup Variations & Substitutions

The beauty of Hot and Sour Soup lies in its adaptability. While the classic version is universally loved, there are many ways to tailor it to your dietary needs or flavor preferences:

*   **Vegetarian/Vegan:** Easily make it vegetarian by using vegetable broth and omitting any meat. For a vegan version, simply ensure your broth is vegetable-based and omit the egg. You can add extra tofu, more varieties of mushrooms (like enoki or oyster mushrooms), or even some rehydrated dried lily flowers for added texture and flavor.
*   **Protein Alternatives:** Instead of pork or chicken, consider using shredded duck, shrimp, or even thinly sliced beef. For a plant-based protein boost, edamame or chickpeas can be a good addition.
*   **Adjusting Spice Levels:** If you prefer less heat, reduce the amount of white pepper. For more fire, add extra chili oil or a pinch of dried chili flakes. Some regional variations use fermented chili bean paste for a deeper, more complex heat.
*   **Sourness Variations:** While rice vinegar is standard, a touch of Chinkiang black vinegar can add a richer, more complex sour note with a hint of sweetness. Experiment to find your preferred balance.
*   **Additional Vegetables:** Feel free to add other quick-cooking vegetables like shredded carrots, snow peas, water chestnuts, or baby corn for extra color and nutrients.
*   **Thickeners:** While cornstarch is common, tapioca starch or arrowroot powder can also be used as thickeners, offering slightly different textures.

Hot and Sour Soup is a testament to the dynamic and delicious world of Chinese cuisine. Its intricate balance of flavors and textures makes it a perennial favorite. Whether you follow a traditional recipe or venture into creative variations, mastering this soup will undoubtedly bring a delightful and authentic taste of China to your kitchen. So, gather your ingredients, fire up the stove, and prepare to embark on a truly satisfying culinary adventure.
